text: This video shows our approach to treatment of complex anal fistulas. This is a case of a forty five year old female patient that presented with high transvecteric fistula as well as anal stenosis. She had symptoms of soiling and constipation that began six months before and were progressively worsening. She had painful defecation and described stool that was very thin. We were unable to do digital rectal examination due to pain and sphincter spasm, so we decided that we would take a closer look. on the operating table while the patient was under anesthesia. As far as other examinations go, she had a colonoscopy done two years earlier that was normal and she had an MRI of the pelvis that showed no other pathology except the earlier mentioned transfectaric fistula. We didn't insist on doing anal monometry because it wouldn't give us any additional information that would change the course of the operation. She obviously didn't have a problem with incontinence, but quite the opposite. We are not sure why this was the case. One of the explanations was that it could have been a reaction from the inflammatory process in the fistula itself.

The surgeon begins the operation by probing the external opening of the fistula and tracing the tract to the internal opening. A retractor is placed in the anal canal, and a metal probe is left in the fistula to aid in dissection. An incision is made at the anocutaneous border, revealing the intersphincteric space and the intersphincteric part of the fistula tract.

The surgeon dissects and ligates the fistula tract adjacent to the internal and external sphincters. The tract is cut between the ligatures, and a fistulascope is inserted through the external opening to explore for secondary branches. Fulguration is performed on the fistula, followed by curettage of necrotic tissue.

The surgical wound is irrigated with hydrogen peroxide and saline solution. The space is closed with resorbable interrupted sutures along the incision on the endocutaneous border. An incision is made on the contralateral side of the anacutaneous border, where the internal sphincter is identified. A small sphincterotomy is performed to ease the anal structure, and the wound is closed with resorbable interrupted sutures.
The surgeon excises the external fistula opening to facilitate healing. The surgical site appears clean, with no signs of fecal incontinence observed.
Thank you very much for your attention and we hope you find this video helpful in your practice.
